sourceFiles.name: requirements
.fixed-models/vehicle-model__review-2026-03-25T10-03-11-487Z__selected-19-issues__2026-03-25T10-40-30-102Z.sysml

fix.title: Clarify cube dimension and reconcile mass limit with 2024 aluminium
fix.description: This fix resolves the requirement-level conflict by replacing the malformed and ambiguous shape statement with an explicit side-length requirement and by updating the mass limit to a physically plausible value for a solid 2024 aluminium cube of that size. The revised requirements are mutually consistent and directly actionable for design and verification.

changes.description: Replaced the ambiguous and physically misleading shape requirement "cube of 1m^2" with a correct dimensional requirement using explicit side length. A 0.05 m cube made of 2024 aluminium is compatible with the existing 1 kg mass cap.

results.name: 05_conflicting_requirements
results.rawText:
Model Characterisation
- System purpose: The main model describes a vehicle system and its operational context, with requirements, structure, behavior, and verification artifacts for driving, startup/shutdown, degraded operation, and some basic performance/safety properties. A separate small `requirements` package defines generic shape/material/weight requirements for an unspecified system.
- Operational domain: Primarily automotive for `VehicleModel`; the standalone `requirements` package is domain-agnostic.
- Model scope: Mixed scope. `VehicleModel` is a partial illustrative system model fragment rather than a complete vehicle specification. The `requirements` package is an isolated requirement fragment.
- Known scope limitations: Many requirements are qualitative or underspecified (`adequate braking performance`, scenario requirements without formal constraints). No detailed timing, protocol, processor allocation, thermal/radiation/vibration, or explicit safety-priority rationale is modeled. Checks depending on such omitted detail are assessed only where direct evidence exists; otherwise they are passed with limitation noted rather than treated as conflicts.

ISSUE [CONFLICT-001]
Element(s):     requirements::Shape; requirements::Weight; requirements::Material
Check:          4. Requirement-Level Conflicts
Finding:        The requirement set in `requirements` is internally incompatible in practice: a "perfect cube of 1m^2" combined with "made of 2024 Aluminium" and "weigh no more than 1kg" yields no physically plausible design if interpreted as a 1 m-scale cube. The geometry/material combination implies a mass far above 1 kg.
Evidence:       `Shape`: "The system shall be a perfect cube of 1m^2."; `Material`: "The system shall be made of 2024 Aluminium."; `Weight`: "The system shall weigh no more than 1kg."
Conflict Type:  Soft
Score:          0.2 - Strong likelihood of driving an impossible or grossly misleading design trade because the material and implied size conflict with the weight cap.
Confidence:     MEDIUM - The conflict depends on interpreting the malformed size statement "cube of 1m^2" as a 1 m-scale geometric requirement; the exact intended dimensional meaning is not formally specified.
Impact:         Physical design space exploration is invalidated because teams cannot choose dimensions and wall/thickness assumptions that satisfy all three requirements as written.
Recommendation: Clarify the shape requirement with correct dimensional semantics (e.g., side length, volume, or surface area), then reconcile it with a realistic mass target for 2024 aluminium or change the material/weight requirement.

CHECK PASSED: 1. Numeric Constraint Conflicts - In `VehicleModel`, examined explicit numeric constraints on `mass`, `maxSpeed`, engine and transmission attributes, wheel dimensions, and power-port bounds; no direct contradictory inequalities on the same attribute were found.

CHECK PASSED: 2. Behavioural Conflicts - Reviewed state transitions, action successions, and scenario flows; no explicit same-trigger overlapping guards, opposite sequencing mandates, or deadline-based contradictions are modeled.

CHECK PASSED: 3. Interface & Protocol Conflicts - Reviewed typed connections among `Driver`, `Vehicle`, `RoadInfrastructure`, `Atmosphere`, and `TrafficSystem`; connected ports are type-compatible and no explicit protocol/rate/direction contradictions are stated.

CHECK PASSED: 4. Requirement-Level Conflicts - Within `VehicleModel`, examined requirement texts and formal constraints for mass, speed, engine, transmission, wheel, interface, and scenario requirements; no direct contradictory requirements on the same vehicle attributes were found.

CHECK PASSED: 5. Allocation Conflicts - The model contains little explicit function-to-physical allocation or processor/resource capacity data, so no allocation conflict can be confirmed from the available text.

CHECK PASSED: 6. Safety vs. Functional Conflicts - Reviewed `SafetyReq`, degraded/safe-state behavior, and driving scenarios; no explicit functional requirement is stated that directly violates a formal safety prohibition.

ISSUE [CONFLICT-002]
Element(s):     VehicleModel::SafetyReq; VehicleModel::DegradedAndSafeStateReq; VehicleModel::Vehicle::OperatingModes; VehicleModel::NominalDriveMission; VehicleModel::DegradedDriveMission
Check:          7. Priority & Resolution
Finding:        The model introduces potentially competing intents—continued operation in degraded mode versus transition to safe state/shutdown for safety—but does not document any priority rule or rationale explaining when safety overrides operational continuity. This leaves an apparent conflict unresolved.
Evidence:       `SafetyReq`: "The vehicle shall have adequate braking performance"; `DegradedAndSafeStateReq`: "The vehicle shall model degraded operation and transition to a safe state when a fault is detected or continued operation is not safe."; state machine includes both `DegradedOperation` and `SafeState`; use case objective for `OperateInDegradedMode`: "Continue limited operation after a fault while maintaining control and preparing for safe recovery."; use case objective for `EnterSafeState`: "Transition the vehicle to a safe state when continued operation is unsafe." No priority ordering, rationale, or conflict resolution annotation is present.
Conflict Type:  Latent
Score:          0.6 - The model may support inconsistent operational decisions in fault conditions because competing intents are present without an explicit resolution policy.
Confidence:     MEDIUM - The tension is evident in the model text, but whether it is a true operational conflict depends on unstated fault-handling policy and author intent.
Impact:         Fault-management design, verification strategy, and safety analysis may produce inconsistent conclusions about whether the vehicle should keep operating or immediately enter safe state.
Recommendation: Add explicit priority/resolution semantics, such as "safety takes precedence over continued degraded operation," with guard conditions defining when degraded operation is allowed and when safe-state transition is mandatory; trace this via `refine`/`satisfy`/rationale relationships.

Summary Table

| ID | Score | Confidence | One-line description |
|---|---:|---|---|
| CONFLICT-001 | 0.2 | MEDIUM | Generic shape/material/weight requirements describe a physically implausible combination. |
| CONFLICT-002 | 0.6 | MEDIUM | Vehicle fault handling lacks explicit priority between degraded operation and safe-state transition. |

Overall Assessment Score: 0.4
Model Status for this Dimension: Critical deficiencies - major rework required before this model can be used
